content.claim: Despite Finland's nominally non-selective comprehensive school structure, SES achievement gaps increased after the 1990s despite having declined initially post-de-tracking, driven by reintroduction of within-school selection mechanisms (specialized language classes, selective class grouping) and school-choice policies that interact with residential segregation, particularly in urban areas like Helsinki.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: Deregulation and school-choice timing in Finland (1990s onward) occurred under different economic and demographic conditions than Hungary faces
Within-school selection mechanisms in Finland operate through different institutional channels (language choice, municipal class grouping) than formal edad-based gimnázium tracks
Finnish teacher professionalism norms and funding equalization may dampen segregation effects relative to Hungary's institutional structure
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
